english: Tar.; sems-càn (sems can) mis (mis) byás-na (byas na) légs-pa (legs pa) gaṅ (gang) yin (yin) which of the actions of human beings are good (in this connection it is nearly the same as bzáṅ-po (bzang po), morally good); legs (legs) is also used in politely hinting or requesting, like the English 'you had better': k'yod (khyod) p'yín-pa (phyin pa) légs(-so) (legs so) Glr., and still more polite: p'yégs-par (phyegs par) legs (legs) your Highness had perhaps better go etc. Pth; nùs-na (nus na) ṡin-tu (shin tu) légs-so (legs so) if you can do it, very well! Dzl.; also légs-so (legs so) alone, very well! well done! légs-so (legs so) légs-so (legs so) excellent! capital! — 2. neat, elegant, graceful, beautiful C. — 3. *lág-mo* (*lag mo*) W. good, due, and adv. well, duly, properly, like légs-par (legs par) (v. above), e.g. *me lág-mo ˌbar ˌdug* (*me lag mo bar dug*) the fire burns well, *lé-ka lág-mo ċos* (*le ka lag mo cos*) you have worked well; but most frq.: clean, pure, clear, *č'u lag-mo* (*chu lag mo*) pure or clear water (opp. to rtsóg-pa (rtsog pa)); fine, of powder, = žíb-mo (zhib mo); *lág-mo ċó-če* (*lag mo co che*) to clean, clear, wash, wipe, sweep etc.; to reduce to fine powder, to pulverize. Comp. légs-ċan (legs can) Sch., legs-ldán (legs ldan) Cs. virtuous (?). — legs-byás (legs byas), resp. legs-mdzád (legs mdzad) good deed, good work Cs. — legs-sbyár (legs sbyar), संस्कृत, well constructed, skilfully arranged, high-wrought, hence: the Sanskrit language. — legs-smón (legs smon) patron, protector, well-wisher, congratulator Cs. — legs-bṡad (legs bshad) a remarkable saying, a sententious remark Mil., two works, called after their authors goṅ-dkár (gong dkar) and sa-skya-legs-bṡád (sa skya legs bshad), are recommended to students of the language. — legs-gsol (legs gsol) resp. thanks, acknowledgment, gratitude C.

(rarely lóṅ-ba (long ba), lón-pa (lon pa)), pf. blaṅs (blangs) (rarely loṅs (longs)), fut. blaṅ (blang), imp. lon (lon) Cs., loṅ(s) (longs) Dzl., Mil., blaṅs (blangs) Cs., W.: *lén-če* (*len che*), *nén-če* (*nen che*), *bláṅ-če* (*blang che*) to take, i.e. 1. to receive, get, obtain, gnas-ṅán (gnas ngan) an inferior place viz. for being re-born Thgy. — 2. to accept, what is offered or given, opp. to ˌdór-ba (dor ba); also to bear, to suffer patiently, to put up with. — 3. to seize, catch, lay hold of, grasp, e.g. one that is about to leap into the water Dzl.; to catch up; to catch, to take prisoner, a culprit Dzl.; to carry off, e. g. the arms of killed enemies; ma byín-par (ma byin par) to take what is not given, to steal, to rob; lén-pa-ˌdra (len pa dra) it is as if it had been stolen from me Glr.; čúṅ-ma lén-pa (chung ma len pa) to get or take a wife, frq., also to procure one for another person; srog lén-pa (srog len pa) = ˌp'róg-pa (phrog pa) to deprive of life, to kill Mng.; to fetch, lén-du (len du) (W. *lén-na-la* (*len na la*)) soṅ (song) go and fetch it! to take possession of, to occupy (by force of arms) Glr.

(Cs. also léb-po (leb po)) flat, srán léb-mo (sran leb mo) Indian pease are flat, lenticular; léb-ċan (leb can), leb-léb (leb leb) id. col., *leb-léb-la bor* (*leb leb la bor*) lay it down flat! léb-ma (leb ma), leb-t'ágs (leb thags) lace, bandage, ribbon Cs., dar-skúd-kyi (dar skud kyi) leb-t'ágs (leb thags) lace of silk thread; b'ag-léb (bag leb) a flat loaf of bread C.; ṡiṅ-léb (shing leb), leb-ṡiṅ (leb shing) board, plank, rdo-léb (rdo leb) a slab, cf. gléb-pa (gleb pa).

division, section of a speech, of a treatise, of a book, chapter, of very different length; léu-ċan (le'u can) Cs., leur byás-pa (le'ur byas pa) Zam. having sections or chapters, being divided into chapters; abbreviated le (le), bṡags-le (bshags le) daṅ (dang) ṡer-le (sher le) ċad-pa (chad pa) yin (yin) the chapters (treating) of the confession of sins and of wisdom are wanting Tar.; lé-ma (le ma) Cs., le-ts'án (le tshan) Sch. id.

1. year (resp. dgúṅ-lo (dgung lo), v. dgúṅ (dgung)), lo lṅa-bċu-pa (lo lnga bcu pa) Ma. usually lo lṅa-bċu lon-pa (lo lnga bcu lon pa) (W. *lon-k'án* (*lon khan*)) fifty years old, of fifty years; bú-mo lo-gnyís-ma (bu mo lo gnyis ma) a girl two years old Ma.; lo daṅs (lo dangs) lo (lo), lo-ré (lo re) (-re (re))-bžin (bzhin), ló-ltar (lo ltar) (Sch. also bstár (bstar) !) annually, yearly; ló-nas ló-ru (lo nas lo ru) from year to year; sṅá-lo (snga lo), ˌdás-lo (das lo) last year; ˌdí-lo (di lo), usually *dá-lo* (*da lo*), this year; p'yí-lo (phyi lo), C. sáṅ-lo (sang lo) next year; lo ˌk'ór-te (lo khor te) after one year had passed, srás-kyis lo k'or-te (sras kyis lo khor te) when the prince was one year old Glr.; the names of the twelve years of the small cycle (v. below) are those of the following twelve animals: byi (byi) mouse, glaṅ (glang) ox, stag (stag) tiger, yos (yos) hare, ˌbrug (brug) dragon, sbrul (sbrul) serpent, rta (rta) horse, lug (lug) sheep, spre (spre) ape, bya (bya) hen, kyi (kyi) dog, p'ag (phag) hog; thus the first year is called byi-ló (byi lo) the mouse-year, and byi-ló-pa (byi lo pa) is a person born in that year etc. — 2. for lo-tóg (lo tog), v. the compounds; for ló-ma (lo ma) leaf, for ló-tsa-ba (lo tsa ba). — 3. prob.: talk, report, rumour, saying, added (like skad (skad)) to the word or sentence to which it belongs, ċe-gé-mo ṡi lo zér-ba t'os tsá-na (che ge mo shi lo zer ba thos tsa na) when a rumour is heard, that N.N. has died
